Title: Emerging trade-offs – impact of photoprotectants (PsbS, xanthophylls, and vitamin E) on oxylipins as regulators of development and defense

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hormonal regulators of development and defense; involved in herbivore/pathogen defense and induction of phloem cell wall reinforcement
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Intrinsic chlorophyll-binding protein of photosystem II
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PsbS increases JA accumulation under herbivory/field conditions and is associated with reduced herbivore attack, indicating PsbS-dependent photoprotection suppresses oxylipin production
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (npq4); field comparison with wild type; jasmonic acid quantification; herbivory assessment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Outdoor/field conditions; herbivory vs no herbivory
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Stronger photoprotection via PsbS may lower oxylipin-mediated biotic defense and increase susceptibility to herbivores, despite benefits for abiotic stress tolerance
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpression to improve abiotic stress tolerance should be evaluated for possible suppression of JA-related defense and increased herbivore susceptibility
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper summarizes published work; PsbS is treated as a photoprotective component rather than enzyme or TF
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PsbS-dependent thermal dissipation suppresses oxylipin accumulation. 2. Arabidopsis npq4 mutants showed higher JA than wild type under herbivory in the field. 3. Increased JA in npq4 correlated with lower herbivore attack, implying a trade-off between abiotic photoprotection and biotic defense.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense/development hormone involved in stress signaling
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Tocopherol cyclase family protein involved in vitamin E biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Vitamin E deficiency caused by vte1 elevates JA accumulation, supporting a suppressive role of tocopherol-dependent detoxification on oxylipin production
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (vte1); JA quantification under stress; comparison with wild type
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light and low temperature; outdoor/field conditions in cited studies
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced vitamin E-mediated abiotic protection may suppress oxylipin signaling and lower biotic defense; deficiency can impair carbon distribution via defensive barricading effects
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering vitamin E pathway for abiotic stress tolerance should consider possible reduction in JA-mediated defense and altered phloem transport/pathogen interactions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although VTE1 is an enzyme, in this review it is used mainly as a mutant marker for vitamin E status affecting oxylipin output rather than as a focal catalytic characterization
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. vte1 mutants lacking tocopherol cyclase accumulate more JA than wild type. 2. Vitamin E-dependent detoxification suppresses oxylipin production. 3. The study highlights a likely trade-off between antioxidant-based abiotic protection and biotic defense signaling.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Oxylipin signals promoting defense-related phloem parenchyma cell wall ingrowths
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Fatty acid desaturases generating polyunsaturated chloroplast fatty acid α-linolenic acid (ALA) precursor
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of FAD7/FAD8 reduces high-light-induced phloem parenchyma wall ingrowths, supporting the requirement for ALA-derived oxylipins in this defense-associated structural response
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant analysis; high-light transfer; electron microscopy quantification of phloem cell wall ingrowths
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transfer from low light to high light
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced precursor supply may limit defense signaling; conversely stronger oxylipin signaling may come with altered growth or carbon allocation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating chloroplast fatty acid desaturation may alter oxylipin-linked structural defenses, but could affect membrane properties and abiotic stress responses
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review uses fad7-1 fad8-1 mainly as evidence that chloroplast fatty acid-derived oxylipins signal wall ingrowth formation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FAD7 and FAD8 produce the ALA precursor pool for oxylipin biosynthesis. 2. fad7-1 fad8-1 mutants had less phloem wall ingrowth under high light than wild type. 3. This supports a signaling role for oxylipins in defense-related wall reinforcement.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream branch step in xanthophyll cycle supplying zeaxanthin for photoprotection
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes de-epoxidation of violaxanthin to antheraxanthin and zeaxanthin in the xanthophyll cycle
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with/suppresses target-product formation indirectly by producing zeaxanthin, which lowers ROS and lipid peroxidation and thereby limits OPDA/JA formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for zeaxanthin formation under excess light
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: npq1 mutants lacking zeaxanthin accumulate more OPDA after high-light transfer and show enhanced defense-associated phloem wall thickening when combined with lutein deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (npq1-1, npq1-2 lut2-1); high-light transfer; OPDA quantification by RP-HPLC and GC/MS; electron microscopy of phloem wall ingrowths
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low-light-grown plants transferred to high light for 7 days
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing zeaxanthin formation may improve abiotic photoprotection but suppress oxylipin-mediated biotic defense
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate for engineering abiotic stress tolerance via enhanced xanthophyll cycling, but deployment should consider possible reduction in oxylipin-based defense traits
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In this review the decisive phenotype is indirect: NPQ1 modulates oxylipin output through photoprotection rather than by catalyzing oxylipin synthesis itself
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NPQ1 encodes violaxanthin de-epoxidase required for zeaxanthin production. 2. Zeaxanthin-deficient npq1 mutants showed elevated OPDA after high-light exposure. 3. Reduced zeaxanthin-mediated photoprotection enhances oxylipin-linked structural defense responses.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Key step in vitamin E biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes a key cyclization step in tocopherol biosynthesis required for vitamin E accumulation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Indirectly suppresses target-product formation by enabling vitamin E accumulation, which detoxifies ROS/lipid peroxy radicals and limits JA formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key step stated in paper; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: vte1 mutants deficient in vitamin E produce more JA than wild type under stress
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; JA quantification; stress comparisons with wild type
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: LOX (inhibited by vitamin E at catalytic iron center, as discussed)
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light and low temperature; field/outdoor conditions in cited reports
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Boosting tocopherol may improve abiotic stress tolerance but may attenuate oxylipin-dependent defense signaling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering target for abiotic stress tolerance and antioxidant capacity, but likely needs balancing with biotic defense traits
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as pathway gene because the review explicitly identifies VTE1 as tocopherol cyclase catalyzing a key vitamin E biosynthetic step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE1 encodes tocopherol cyclase in vitamin E biosynthesis. 2. Loss of VTE1 elevates JA accumulation. 3. Vitamin E synthesis suppresses oxylipin formation by detoxifying reactive species.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling; induction of phloem cell wall reinforcement
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Fatty acid desaturase 7; Fatty acid desaturase 8
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Chloroplast fatty acid desaturation supplying oxylipin biosynthesis precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Chloroplast fatty acids leading to trienoic fatty acid formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduce desaturation in chloroplast fatty acids to generate polyunsaturated fatty acids including ALA, the precursor for oxylipin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for OPDA and JA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Precursor-supply step; key control not directly established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: fad7-1 fad8-1 mutants exhibit reduced high-light-induced phloem wall ingrowth, supporting reduced oxylipin signaling from loss of ALA precursor production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant analysis; high-light transfer; ultrastructural phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transfer from low light to high light
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing chloroplast fatty acid desaturation could alter both membrane properties and defense signaling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point to tune oxylipin precursor supply, but likely pleiotropic for membrane function and stress responses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used in the review as evidence linking chloroplast fatty acid precursor availability to defense-related structural outcomes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FAD7/FAD8 provide ALA precursor for oxylipin biosynthesis. 2. The fad7 fad8 double mutant suppresses high-light-induced phloem wall ingrowth. 3. Oxylipin precursor supply is required for this defense-associated response.
